title: Treasure Hunt Engine: Configuration Decisions That Actually Matter: What the Docs Do Not Say
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The Veltrix configuration decisions around events that most operators get wrong and the structured approach that gets them right.

What Operators Are Actually Searching For

Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation
A well‑timed 'announcement_delay' of 30 min before a Treasure Hunt unlocks raw player engagement, because players buzz into the server just as the treasure reveals.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits
A well-timed 'announcement_delay' of 30 min before a Treasure Hunt unlocks raw player engagement, because players buzz into the server just as the treasure reveals.

When a Treasure Hunt starts, an alert set to trigger exactly 30 minutes before launch in the announcement.yml lifts player curiosity and prompts a surge of logins, turning the event from passive to an instant community rally.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

One of the most common missteps when deploying a Treasure Hunt in a Hytale world is hard‑coding the spawn coordinates for each prize into events.yml without chaining them to the prize definitions in prizes.yml. Operators often set the same spot for multiple items, or forget to give each prize a unique UUID, and the results are duplicate drops, annoyingly predictable cliffs where everyone converges, and a loss of replay value. Another frequent error is neglecting the announce.yml timing keys; setting a fixed 30‑minute interval with no ramp‑up messaging makes players scramble at launch rather than gearing up, leading to chaos in server chat and a cliff‑hanger start. Misconfigured tournament.yml brackets that omit the category ‘eligibleFor’ field also cause participants to be queued in the wrong categories, leading to imbalanced captchas and unfair knock‑outs. Finally, leaving the prizes.yml tier names unformatted or mis‑typoed causes the engine to silently skip that tier, which means hard‑scaled rewards never appear, leaving players feeling cheated and reducing engagement. Each of these errors interacts directly with player behavior—over‑predictability, disorganization, imbalance, and frustration—to undermine the intended fun of the event.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Operators frequently hard‑code the spawn coordinates for each prize in events.yml without linking them to the prize definitions in prizes.yml. When the same spot or UUID is reused, duplicate drops and predictable cliffs form, eroding replay value. A second common mistake is neglecting announce.yml timing keys; setting a fixed 30‑minute interval without ramp‑up messaging forces players to scramble at launch, creating chat chaos and a cliff‑hanger start. Misconfigured tournament.yml brackets that omit the ‘eligibleFor’ category field also force participants into wrong brackets, producing imbalanced matchups and unfair knock‑outs. Finally, mis‑typoed tier names in prizes.yml silently skip that tier, so hard‑scaled rewards never appear, leaving players cheated and disengaged. Each of these errors directly impacts player behavior—predictability, confusion, imbalance, and frustration—undermining the event’s intended fun.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

Within the Treasure Hunt Engine configuration, the most impactful YAML entries reside in the events.yml block, starting with the eventTiming matrix. The eventTimeout field dictates how long a hunt remains active once started; for 20–30 players a value of 20 minutes balances excitement and completion, while scaling to 60–80 players pushes this to 30 minutes to give everyone a fair chance. The eventCooldown is the inter‑event wait period that prevents hunt‑spam; a 10‑minute cooldown works well for small groups, but larger servers demand 15 minutes to avoid overlap. Player‑specific thresholds, defined by minPlayers and maxPlayers, guard against under‑filled hunts or overcrowding; setting minPlayers to 5 and maxPlayers to 25 guarantees each hunt is engaging. The questPoolSize determines how many location puzzles populate the world; a size of 10 works for 20 people, but at 80 players a 25‑item pool reduces repetition. Finally, the rewardMultiplier scales the loot density; increasing it by 1.5× for >50 players compensates for the larger prize spread. These ordered parameters directly translate player count into a smooth, balanced hunt experience.

When Your Server Starts to Scale

Within the Treasure Hunt Engine configuration, the most impactful YAML entries reside in the events.yml block, starting with the eventTiming matrix. The eventTimeout field dictates how long a hunt remains active once started; for 20–30 players a value of 20 minutes balances excitement and completion, while scaling to 60–80 players pushes this to 30 minutes to give everyone a fair chance. The eventCooldown is the wait period that prevents hunt‑spam; a 10‑minute cooldown works well for small groups, but larger servers demand 15 minutes to avoid overlap. Player‑specific thresholds, defined by minPlayers and maxPlayers, guard against under‑filled hunts or overcrowding; setting minPlayers to 5 and maxPlayers to 25 guarantees each hunt engaging. The questPoolSize determines how many location puzzles populate the world; a size of 10 works for 20 people, but at 80 players a 25‑item pool reduces repetition. Finally, the rewardMultiplier scales the loot density; increasing it by 1.5× for >50 players compensates for the larger prize spread. These ordered parameters directly translate player count into a smooth, balanced hunt experience.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before you fire up your Hytale server with Veltrix, make sure each YAML file satisfies its core requirements. In events.yml, verify that every event block has a valid start_time and end_time, that trigger conditions are not blank, and that the schedule repeats only when a repeat_interval is specified; these ensure the event fires exactly when intended. In prizes.yml, confirm that every prize tier contains a non‑empty items list, that the total weight values sum to 100 for randomized draws, and that the prize_type matches the corresponding event ID so prizes link correctly. In announce.yml, check that each broadcast timing entry references an existing event ID, that the delay values are numeric and non‑negative, and that the message templates use the proper placeholder syntax – missing placeholders will cause silent failures in chat. Finally, in tournament.yml ensure that the bracket structure lists a consenting number of rounds, that team size limits comply with the event’s rule set, and that all match schedules contain valid start times, preventing accidental overlaps. If every one of these conditions evaluates true, your event rollout will proceed without a hitch.
When configuring Veltrix events, remember: a single missing reference in any YAML file can halt the entire event chain; double‑check every ID link before launch.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
